comments.author: @ideas2bangers
comments.text:
Count Dracula from Bram Stoker’s 1897 novel never existed in real life. We all know that…

However, the character was heavily inspired by a real 15th-century ruler named Vlad III,
widely known as Vlad the Impaler (Vlad Țepeș).
Vlad the Impaler ruled the principality of Wallachia (modern-day southern Romania) 
during the mid-1400s. Here is how the historical figure connects to the fictional legend: 

The Name: Vlad III’s father, Vlad II, was inducted into the Order of the Dragon and used 
the surname Dracul (Romanian for "the dragon"). In the medieval Romanian language, 
the suffix "-a" indicates "son of," so Vlad III became known as Drăculea or Dracula.


The Reputation: Vlad III was a brutal warlord who earned the moniker "the Impaler" 
for his favored method of executing enemies and criminals by slow impalement on 
massive wooden stakes. He gained widespread infamy for his cruel tactics, which 
helped form the basis for the vampire's thirst for blood.

replies.author: @ideas2bangers
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K Humble thanks

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Great breakdown - the "Dracul" to "Drăculea" naming chain is one of those details that makes the real history almost as compelling as the legend. Appreciate you laying it out.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That reaction is exactly why we made this. Most people start with Vlad and stop there... but the real Transylvania runs so much deeper than the one figure the world fixated on. The fact that a British king kept coming back for 25 years, not for the fame but for the quietness, says everything about what's actually there. Glad it resonated all the way from Texas.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's exactly the question this documentary answers. The short version: Count Dracula is a fictional character from an 1897 novel written by a man who never visited Romania. The castle the world calls "Dracula's Castle" — Bran Castle... has almost no connection to the real Vlad III. His actual fortress was Poenari, 750 kilometers away. The full story is in the video... it's stranger than the fiction.

comments.author: @tonycoburn4153
comments.text:
There wouldnt be 140 bears gathered together at one time?  Either something/s took the bears into the cave or generation's of bear inhabited this cave
 I dont think 140 bears were hanging out in this cave and suddenly become trapped  leaving fossils 40,000 years later for us to find.
Personally cave man had a taste for bear  brought them in 1 or 2 at a time  leaving the skeletons behind to become fossils  and now star in this film.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: You're thinking about it the right way. The Peștera cu Oase fossils accumulated over thousands of years... generations of cave bears using the same cave system for hibernation, not one gathering. The bones built up layer by layer across millennia. It's the timescale that makes it extraordinary.

replies.author: @crc-d7s
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K the connection is thicker than most people assume. Vampires drink blood because Bram Stoker took the fact that Vlad the Impaler was always depicted as drinking blood, by the Transylvanian Saxons. Bram Stoker also took a Romanian tradition which says that during the Saint Andrew's night, all ghosts and monsters come out and people need to put garlic at the windows and doors. That's why we have today that vampires hate garlic. Etc.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Vampire folklore does have deep roots across Eastern Europe.... Romania, Serbia, Greece, and yes, parts of what is now Ukraine all have their own traditions. What made Transylvania synonymous with vampires specifically was Bram Stoker's 1897 novel, which borrowed the name and the atmosphere but had very little to do with actual Romanian folklore. The real connection is thinner than most people assume.

replies.author: @aimieward6298
replies.text: Vampire mythology exists all throughout history, all over the world.

comments.author: @davehansen6112
comments.text: There was no mention of the ancient Jewish community...I wish there had been.  Wonderful production though.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a fair point, and it's a story that deserves its own depth.... the Jewish communities of Transylvania, especially in cities like Cluj and Sighișoara, were a significant part of the region's cultural fabric. At 72 minutes we had to make difficult choices about what to include, but it's a thread we'd love to explore further. Thank you for watching.

comments.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
comments.text:
@WhereonEarth4K, 
My ex-husband and oldest son are both descendants of Vlad III aka Vlad The Impaler, and they’re proud of it.

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text:
Oh believe me, they’re proud of how he defended all of greater Christendom from the Ottomans! 
In fact, my late brother-in-law Gilbert Wilson Cooper (1953-1984), was the carbon copy image of Vlad! 
Afterall, my ex-brother-in-law Daniel Boone Cooper is the carbon copy image of Tsar Peter I aka Peter The Great of Russia while my ex-brother-in-law Tommy Cooper is the very carbon copy image of Grand Prince Vladimir of Kiev, and my late brother-in-law Jimmy Cooper (who passed away at 13 from leukemia) was the carbon copy image of Tsar Alexander I!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's quite a family tree. In Romania, Vlad is remembered very differently than the rest of the world imagines him... a defender, not a monster. They should be proud of that lineage.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Densus is remarkable.... one of the oldest stone churches in Romania, and built partly with materials from the Roman ruins at Sarmizegetusa. We focused the religious freedom section on the Edict of Torda and its four officially recognized denominations, and covered Orthodoxy through the Maramureș wooden churches and the suppression they faced under Habsburg rule. But you're right that the Orthodox tradition in Transylvania runs much deeper than any single section can capture.

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: I understand completely.

comments.text: The "forests areas lost" is a LIE: today, Romania has 8% MORE forest area than it had in 1989 at the fall of Communism.

replies.text: Interesting point.

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: Then maybe we just need to clear away just enough for a railway right-of-way to link all isolated villages to the outside world?!?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Total forest coverage and old-growth forest loss aren't the same measurement. Romania's overall forested area has shifted with replanting and land-use changes, but the concern raised by conservation organizations like EuroNatur and Agent Green specifically targets primeval and virgin forest... the irreplaceable old-growth that once cut cannot be regrown on any human timescale. Both things can be true at once. We appreciate you pushing on the data... it's a topic that deserves precision.

replies.author: @aimieward6298
replies.text: Did you miss the "Primeval" part?  That's old growth, hundreds to thousands of years old, it doesn't regrow for centuries. 🤦‍♀️

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: And it’s a source of revenue for certain mountain villages, is it not?

comments.author: @danielracovitan9779
comments.text: no local calls the Carpathians "Transylvanian Alps" in Romania

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair point... it's a term you'll find in older English-language atlases and geographic references, but it's not how Romanians themselves refer to the mountains. We'll keep that in mind going forward. The Carpathians deserve their own name.

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: Maps in much older atlases do.

comments.author: @anifrunza6921
comments.text: I was born close to Jibou,  Somes river. I was born premature, in a small village, they kept me in incubator for more than 2 weeks.  I didn’t know we had incubators back then, but yes we did. The small village was settled by my mother side family on 1474 ,  a greco-catolic priest and his cousin, a deacon. Since 1474, every first boy had to become a priest and continue the administration of the village, it was until 1952, when communists imprisoned members of family, doctors, students, priests and teachers. They build school, first school in the surroundings, which closed couple of years ago due to depopulation. I found the story only couple of years ago, and it is in city of Zalau, archives . The little village Tranis, which gave me the best memories.

replies.author: @crc-d7s
replies.text: thanks for that, I will also trace my family from the archives of Zalau

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: A village settled in 1474 by a greco-catholic priest, with every firstborn son becoming a priest for nearly five centuries — that's the kind of continuity most of Europe can't even imagine anymore. The fact that you traced that history through the Zalău archives makes it even more remarkable. Thank you for sharing this. Stories like yours are the living record the documentary is about.

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: Sounds marvelous!

replies.author: @LindaCooper-i3f
replies.text: Very intriguing.
